story:
After “The Meeting,” Leo bolts to his office, crams his briefcase and pockets with what matters, dumps cold coffee across his keyboard, and flings the company mug at the founder’s portrait. Shards everywhere. Gossip will fly. He has to get to Irene—first—before anyone else’s version poisons what he still hopes can be saved.

He rips off his ID—Leo Gulliby, HR Coordinator, Rubagub Robotics—and hammers for the elevator, then rethinks: a moving coffin of coworkers, their mouths sizzling rumor. Stairs. Through the lobby. Nora, the receptionist he hired, files purple nails, blows a pink bubble, and needles him with sugar-coated barbs about “The Meeting.” “You can fake a taste, not your love, right? How was that roast goose you had?” she sing-songs without looking up. He snaps back, storms outside into flensing wind and snow.

The company grounds lie in a seamless silk of white, the immaculate lawn that even geese no longer trespass. He and Irene ate roast goose once—he pretended to like it for her. He can fake a taste, not his love. Irene will understand, he tells himself. She must. The laws are wrong, not them. They could be the ones to change things.

His Chevy Malibu is the lone car still blanketed in snow. So the lot boys already know he’s Not Important. He scrapes the windows, shakes, drives. Ten floors of Rubagub—red as a scab—recede in the mirror. He curses the place that also gave him Irene: the Christmas party bartender with blue, impossible eyes and a champagne voice. He’s certain the Dispatchers are coming—Mr. Nidd, Mr. Drover, the twin Binnacles. They’ll bring the Confidentiality Agreement, the Termination Form, and the cold procedure that leaves no room for pleas. If he reaches her first, apologizes, they can stand together. Stop it.

He guns through a white blur on an empty late-morning highway, twenty-two miles to their secret white clapboard house among trees, the nursery waiting though no baby is on the way, the crib he built, the tiny booties Irene knits. He almost calls, decides face-to-face is better. Then her face fills the car screen, lit like ice.

“They’re already here,” Irene says. “They’re in the nursery. I asked them to pack it up first.”

He chokes. Divorce, he says. Their only crime is marrying—he didn’t want to, but she—

“Don’t put this on me,” she says. “You promised me children. I wasn’t having babies without marriage. You made me believe I could have babies with you.”

He offers adoption. He insists it wasn’t deception; it was love. Her stare does not blink. “I wanted my own babies. With you.”

The truth slams him: her love isn’t enough, or not anymore; motherhood matters more. “I’ll sign whatever they bring,” she says. “We’re over. Don’t come home.”

The call fades. Wheels judder. Trees bloom across the windshield.

Later, Rubagub’s cleanup crew arrives. Titanium claws pry apart the crumpled Malibu. “Fifth time in two years a synth tried to fake it with a human,” one bot says. “You make ’em look human, of course they’ll want all the bennies.”

Another lifts Leo’s head from the glass. Fiber-optic tendrils dangle like tinsel; he tucks them gently back. They box the parts. Someone worries about layoffs if the synth line gets axed; another snorts—robots will always do the grunt work humans won’t.

The last bot lingers, vacuuming fragments. “You synths,” he mutters. “You act human, eat and shiver like humans, think that makes you human. But there’s one thing you can’t do—create life—and when you try to fake that, this is what happens.” The crate rides away, sealed, the road whitening behind it.
